quiz_questions.question_text: A 2 kg ball moves in a circle of radius 1.5 m at 6 m/s. What is the centripetal force?
A 1500 kg car rounds a flat curve of radius 100 m at 20 m/s. What centripetal force is needed?
A 0.5 kg ball on a string moves in a horizontal circle of radius 0.8 m with a period of 0.5 s. What is the tension in the string?
What provides the centripetal force for a satellite orbiting Earth?
quiz_questions.explanation: For an orbiting satellite, gravitational attraction provides the centripetal force needed to maintain the circular orbit.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 70 kg person on a Ferris wheel of radius 12 m feels a normal force of 500 N at the top. What is the Ferris wheel's speed? (g = 10 m/s²)
What centripetal force acts on a 0.1 kg coin sitting on a turntable at radius 0.2 m, rotating at 2 rev/s?
A car goes over a hill with radius of curvature 20 m. At what speed does the car lose contact with the road? (g = 10 m/s²)
quiz_questions.explanation: At the top, N = 0 when mg = mv²/r, so v = sqrt(rg) = sqrt(200) ≈ 14.1 m/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 3 kg object moves in a vertical circle of radius 2 m. At the top, the string tension is 10 N. What is the speed at the top? (g = 10 m/s²)
A banked track has an angle of 45° and radius 100 m. What speed requires no friction? (g = 10 m/s²)
quiz_questions.explanation: v = sqrt(rg tanθ) = sqrt(100 × 10 × tan45°) = sqrt(1000) ≈ 31.6 m/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Moon orbits Earth at a distance of 3.84 × 10⁸ m with a period of 27.3 days. What is the centripetal acceleration?
What is centripetal force?

options.text: The force that speeds up objects in circular motion
A specific type of force, like gravity or friction
A force that acts outward from the center
The net force directed toward the center of the circular path that causes circular motion: F_c = mv²/r

quiz_questions.explanation: Centripetal force is not a new type of force; it is the net inward force that maintains circular motion. It can be provided by gravity, tension, friction, normal force, or any combination.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'centripetal' literally mean?
quiz_questions.explanation: 'Centripetal' comes from Latin roots meaning 'center-seeking.' The centripetal force is always directed toward the center of the circular path.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the formula for centripetal force?

options.text: F_c = mv²r, directed toward the center
F_c = mv/r², directed away from the center
F_c = mv²/r = mω²r, directed toward the center of the circle
F_c = mrω, directed tangent to the circle

quiz_questions.explanation: Centripetal force F_c = mv²/r = mω²r, always directed radially inward toward the center of the circular path.

quiz_questions.question_text: What provides the centripetal force for a car turning on a flat road?

options.text: The normal force from the road
Static friction between the tires and the road

quiz_questions.explanation: On a flat road, static friction between the tires and road surface provides the inward centripetal force needed for the car to follow the curved path.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the purpose of banking a curve?

options.text: To increase the speed limit on the curve
To reduce the weight of vehicles on the curve
To tilt the normal force so its horizontal component provides centripetal force, reducing reliance on friction

quiz_questions.explanation: Banking tilts the normal force inward, so its horizontal component provides (or assists with) the centripetal force. At the design speed, no friction is needed.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'critical speed' at the top of a vertical loop?

options.text: The speed at which centripetal force equals gravity
The minimum speed at the top where the normal force (or tension) just becomes zero: v_c = sqrt(rg)
The speed at which friction provides all the centripetal force
The maximum speed before the track breaks

quiz_questions.explanation: At critical speed, gravity alone provides the centripetal force (N = 0): mg = mv²/r, giving v_c = sqrt(rg). Below this speed, the object loses contact.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a conical pendulum?

options.text: A pendulum with a cone-shaped bob
A mass on a string that sweeps out a cone as it moves in a horizontal circle
A pendulum that swings in an ellipse

quiz_questions.explanation: A conical pendulum has a mass on a string that traces a horizontal circle, with the string tracing the surface of a cone. The horizontal component of tension provides centripetal force.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'design speed' of a banked curve mean?

options.text: The speed at which the horizontal component of the normal force alone provides all the centripetal force (no friction needed)
The average speed of traffic on the curve under most circumstances involving speed
The speed at which the car would skid, even when conditions shift significantly
The maximum safe speed for the curve according to widely held views on this topic

quiz_questions.explanation: The design speed v = sqrt(rg tanθ) is the speed where banking alone provides the centripetal force, requiring zero friction.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens to centripetal force if speed doubles while radius stays the same?
quiz_questions.explanation: F_c = mv²/r. Since F_c ∝ v², doubling v makes F_c four times as large.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the role of the normal force in circular motion on a vertical loop?

options.text: It is always equal to the weight
It is always directed toward the center and varies with position in the loop
It only acts at the top and bottom

quiz_questions.explanation: In a vertical loop, the normal force is always perpendicular to the surface (toward center) and varies: largest at the bottom (N = mg + mv²/r) and smallest at the top (N = mv²/r - mg).

quiz_questions.question_text: A 1200 kg car enters a dip in the road with radius of curvature 30 m at 15 m/s. What is the normal force on the car at the bottom of the dip? (g = 10 m/s²)

A spin cycle of a top-loading washing machine has a drum radius of 0.3 m. The drum spins at 800 rpm. A wet T-shirt (0.3 kg) is pressed against the wall. What normal force does the drum exert on the shirt?
A highway curve is banked at 12° with radius 300 m. A car traveling at 30 m/s rounds the curve. Is friction needed, and if so, in which direction? (g = 10 m/s²)

options.text: Yes, friction is needed up the incline (car tends to slide outward/upward)
Yes, friction is needed down the incline (car tends to slide inward)

quiz_questions.explanation: Design speed: v_d = sqrt(300(10)tan12°) = sqrt(637) ≈ 25.2 m/s. Since 30 > 25.2, the car moves faster than the design speed, so friction must point up the incline (inward) to provide additional centripetal force.

quiz_questions.question_text: An astronaut training centrifuge has a radius of 8 m. At what angular velocity does the astronaut experience 6g? (g = 9.8 m/s²)
quiz_questions.explanation: 6g = ω²r, so ω = sqrt(6g/r) = sqrt(6(9.8)/8) = sqrt(7.35) ≈ 2.71 rad/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 0.4 kg ball attached to a 1.2 m string is swung in a vertical circle. At the bottom of the arc, the speed is 8 m/s. What is the tension in the string? (g = 10 m/s²)
A highway engineer must design a curve for a speed of 25 m/s. If the maximum allowable centripetal acceleration for passenger comfort is 2 m/s², what minimum radius is needed?
A coin sits on a turntable 0.1 m from the center. The turntable gradually speeds up. If μ_s = 0.3, at what angular velocity does the coin slip? (g = 10 m/s²)
quiz_questions.explanation: μ_s mg = mω²r. ω = sqrt(μ_s g/r) = sqrt(0.3(10)/0.1) = sqrt(30) ≈ 5.48 rad/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: A motorcycle stunt rider rides on the inside wall of a vertical cylinder of radius 5 m. What minimum speed is needed if μ_s = 0.6? (g = 10 m/s²)
quiz_questions.explanation: Normal force provides centripetal force: N = mv²/r. Friction supports weight: μ_s N = mg. So μ_s(mv²/r) = mg. v = sqrt(rg/μ_s) = sqrt(5(10)/0.6) = sqrt(83.3) ≈ 9.13 m/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: A roller coaster car (500 kg) enters a vertical loop of radius 8 m. At the top of the loop, passengers feel 'weightless.' What is the car's speed at the top? (g = 10 m/s²)
quiz_questions.explanation: 'Weightless' means N = 0, so gravity provides all centripetal force: mg = mv²/r, v = sqrt(rg) = sqrt(80) ≈ 8.94 m/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: A bucket of water is swung in a vertical circle of radius 1 m. What minimum speed at the top prevents the water from falling out? (g = 10 m/s²)
quiz_questions.explanation: At minimum speed, gravity provides the full centripetal force: mg = mv²/r, v = sqrt(rg) = sqrt(10) ≈ 3.16 m/s.
